title: Friends with Health Benefits: A Field Experiment
abstract: When pursuing goals, we commonly choose between going it alone versus teaming up together. In a field experiment (n= 774), we tested the benefits of rewarding individual versus tandem goal pursuit. In astandard-rewardcondition, we experimentally offered gym members an individual cash reward each day they visited the gym for four weeks. Participants in atandem-rewardcondition could earn the same reward but only if they surmounted an extra logistical hurdle: they had to visit the gym with a friend. Although this additional requirement made it more difficult for participants in thetandem-rewardcondition to earn equivalent incentives, participants with this extra hurdle visited the gym about 35% more frequently than those earning a standard reward. A follow-up survey suggests that tandem rewards provide nonmonetary incentives that change behavior, including increased accountability and enjoyment. Our findings illustrate the advantages of making desired behaviors social to promote follow-through.

author.Rachel Gershon: Haas School of Business, University of California, Berkeley, Berkeley, California 94720;
author.Cynthia Cryder: Olin Business School, Washington University, St. Louis, Missouri 63130;
author.Katherine L. Milkman: The Wharton School University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19104

article.Methods: A preregistered field experiment tested whether rewarding individuals for attending the gym with a friend increases gym attendance more than simply rewarding individual gym attendance. We partnered with the on-campus gyms at a large public university in California to conduct this experiment. All undergraduate and graduate students at the university were eligible to participate and had free access to the campus gyms. We recruited participants through fliers at the on-campus fitness centers and an email to a research participation listserv (see ResearchBox for recruitment materials). Based on a pilot study, we determined that we would need at least 278 participants per condition to be 80% powered to detect a 25% difference in exercise between conditions at an alpha level of 0.05. Our final sample size was determined by the number of participants that we could recruit prior to the start date. Interested students could visit our customized website, on which they first saw the informed consent document. They could then sign up for the study with their name and university email address as well as the name and university email address of one friend. Potential participants were not given details about the incentive structure in the two conditions and, therefore, did not know that payment may be conditional on visiting the gym with this friend. Their friend—hereafter referred to as their gym buddy—then received an email with the informed consent document. If a registrant’s gym buddy also consented to participate, then both students were enrolled in our study. Note that we carefully designed this study to isolate and test the effect of tandem rewards. Participants in both conditions signed up for the study with a friend, thereby holding constant the effects of social pairing and varying only the incentive structure for (tandem) goal pursuit. All participant pairs were randomly assigned to one of two experimental conditions (i.e., gym buddies were always assigned to the same condition as each other): either thestandard-rewardor thetandem-rewardcondition. On January 28, 2020, participants received instructions (see ResearchBox for the instruction email) for participating in “Gym Bonus Month,” which lasted for four weeks (February 1–28, 2020), during which they could earn rewards for using the on-campus gyms. Participants in both conditions were offered a $1 Amazon gift card for each gym visit (up to a maximum of once per day), but participants in thetandem-rewardcondition were required to arrive at the gym within 30 minutes of their gym buddy to earn this incentive. Participants in all conditions were also told that they could only receive their incentive payment if they scheduled their gym visit at least an hour beforehand on our study website and showed up to the gym within 30 minutes of their scheduled time. Whereas previous work finds that scheduling and advanced planning can have a positive effect on some behaviors (Milkman et al. 2011), research has not shown a positive benefit of planning exercise (Carrera et al. 2018). Still, as a conservative approach, we added this scheduling requirement to hold constant across conditions any potential benefits of scheduling workouts ahead of time because scheduling is necessary when coordinating as a pair but not when exercising alone. To schedule gym visits on our website, participants selected one-hour time slots when they planned to visit the gym. There was no penalty if they failed to show up at the scheduled time and participants could schedule multiple appointments at once. After making an appointment, participants received an email reminder and were given the option to add the appointment to their calendar (see ResearchBox link). After randomization and after instructions were sent, one individual asked to be removed from the study. We removed this participant and the participant’s gym buddy, which left us with 774 participants (387 buddy pairs). Participants were 60.59% female (for comparison, the listserv from which we recruited participants was 64% female according to the 2,043 participants on this listserv who completed a prescreening in the two years prior to our experiment). Participants went to the gym, on average, 0.74 times each week in the 10 weeks leading up to the start of our study. Participants completed 20.51% of all gym visits during this preintervention period in tandem with their gym buddy (meaning both participants entered the gym during the same 30-minute interval). We found no significant differences between conditions in participant characteristics measured before the intervention (seeTable 1). Table 1.Comparing Participants Across Conditions Table 1.Comparing Participants Across Conditions Note.Standard deviations in parentheses. Dependent Variables Following past research, we used data on access card swipes to track gym visits and measure the effect of our experimental manipulation on gym attendance (Charness and Gneezy 2009,Acland and Levy 2015,Royer et al. 2015). The primary outcome of interest in this study was the total number of gym visits (up to one per day) that each participant made per week whether the visit was scheduled. We also measured a binary outcome capturing whether a participant made any gym visits in a given week (1 = yes, 0 = no). We analyzed these dependent variables during the four-week intervention period and for the two weeks postintervention.1We also included 10 weeks of preintervention observations in our analysis to control for each participant’s baseline gym usage and increase statistical power. To evaluate the effect of assignment to our treatment condition, we ran the following ordinary least squares regression model, which amounts to a difference-in-differences estimate of the impact of our treatment (assignment to thetandem-rewardcondition) on participants’ weekly exercise: Standard errors in this model are clustered by gym buddy dyad. In this model,iindexes participants andtindexes weeks. The dependent variable,𝑦𝑖⁢𝑡yi⁢tis the number of gym visits made by participantiin weekt(or for the binary model, an indicator for whether participantivisited the gym in weekt). On the right-hand side of the equation is our primary predictor: an indicator for whether participantiwas assigned to our tandem-reward condition (treatmenti) interacted with an indicator for whether weektis during Gym Bonus Month (interventionweekt; 1 = yes, 0 = no). The right-hand side of the equation also includes week fixed effects (𝑍𝑡Zt) and participant fixed effects (𝑋𝑖Xi), which account for a participant’s treatment condition and gym attendance during the 10-week baseline period prior to Gym Bonus Month.B1in our model estimates the average effect of our treatment during Gym Bonus Month after accounting for individual differences in preintervention gym attendance with participant fixed effects.
article.Results: During the intervention period, participants across conditions visited the gym an average of 1.07 times per week (0.39 more visits per week, on average, than in the 10 weeks preintervention).Table 1displays participants’ gender, baseline gym attendance preintervention, and average weekly gym visits during Gym Bonus Month and the two-week postintervention period by condition.Figure 1plots week-by-week gym attendance across conditions, subtracting participants’ baseline gym attendance prior to treatment to create a standard reference across conditions. AsTable 1andFigure 1illustrate, participants increased their gym visits in thestandard-rewardcondition compared with baseline during the intervention, but as predicted, they increased their gym attendance even more in thetandem-rewardcondition. During the intervention, participants in thetandem-rewardcondition made about 35% more gym visits than those in thestandard-rewardcondition. Table 2presents the results of our key preregistered test of whether tandem rewards increase gym attendance more than standard rewards. AsTable 2, Model 1, shows, during the intervention period, participants in thetandem-reward(versusstandard-reward) condition visited the gym a regression-estimated 0.23 more times per week (SE= 0.100,p= 0.023).Table 2, Model 2, shows a nonsignificant difference between conditions on the binary outcome (any gym visits in a given week;b= 0.041,SE= 0.028,p= 0.135). This suggests that our treatment effect primarily arises on the intensive margin; tandem rewards increased participants’ frequency of gym visits but did not significantly increase their likelihood of visiting the gym in a given week (admittedly, this is also a less sensitive measure of exercise frequency). We found no significant heterogeneity in our results by whether participants visited the gym more or less than the median person in our study prior to Gym Bonus Month (b= −0.104,SE= 0.179,p= 0.562). We also find that the results do not differ significantly based on gender using a three-way interaction between the treatment, intervention period, and a female indicator:b= 0.147,SE= 0.172,p= 0.394. Table 2.Regression-Estimated Effect of Assignment to theTandem-RewardCondition on Gym Attendance During Gym Bonus Month Table 2.Regression-Estimated Effect of Assignment to theTandem-RewardCondition on Gym Attendance During Gym Bonus Month Notes.This table reports the results of ordinary least squares regressions predicting participants’ weekly gym visits (Model 1) and likelihood of visiting the gym at least once in a given week (Model 2) during our four-week intervention period. Both models include an indicator for assignment to thetandem-rewardcondition interacted with an indicator for our four-week intervention period, participant fixed effects (which account for participants’ experimental condition), and calendar week fixed effects (which account for the four-week intervention period). Robust standard errors are clustered by gym buddy dyads. The omitted reference group is thestandard-rewardcondition. **p< 0.05. Social Engagement In addition to estimating the impact of our treatment on gym attendance, we evaluated whether participants were more likely to visit the gym with their gym buddy in thetandem-rewardcondition. In the 10 weeks prior to the intervention period, the rate of simultaneous gym visits (i.e., gym buddies swiping into the gym within 30 minutes of each other) did not differ by condition (tandem-rewardcondition = 21.10%,standard-rewardcondition = 21.83%,χ2(1) = 0.38,p= 0.54; seeFigure 2). However, during Gym Bonus Month, we found that a significantly higher rate of gym visits occurred simultaneously in thetandem-rewardcondition (45.29% of gym visits) than in thestandard-rewardcondition (27.61%,χ2(1) = 106.82,p< 0.001). In short, tying an incentive to simultaneous goal pursuit nearly doubled participants’ rate of exercising with their gym buddy. Scheduling In thestandard-rewardcondition, participants scheduled a total of 821 gym visits or an average of 0.54 per week, whereas participants in thetandem-rewardcondition scheduled a total of 1,019 gym visits or a higher average of 0.65 scheduled gym visits per week (z= 4.06,p< 0.001). After scheduling, there was a nonsignificant difference in follow-through between the two conditions. Specifically, 68.79% of scheduled appointments led to a scheduled gym visit in thetandem-rewardcondition and 72.23% in thestandard-rewardcondition (χ2(1) = 2.55,p= 0.11). The higher rate of scheduling in thetandem-rewardcondition may reflect a desire to signal good intentions to a gym buddy or greater anticipated enjoyment. We caution against drawing conclusions from the higher rate of scheduled visits or the nonsignificant difference in follow-through because the absence of a penalty for nonattendance might have influenced participants to over-schedule their visits, optimizing their chances of receiving payment rather than aligning with their genuine intention to attend. Postintervention Period We observed a nonsignificant difference in gym attendance between our two study conditions in the two-week postintervention period in terms of both how often participants visited the gym (b= 0.021,SE= 0.078,p= 0.79) and how likely they were to visit the gym in a given week (b= −0.012,SE= 0.027,p= 0.649). Unfortunately, the COVID-19 pandemic likely influenced participants’ behavior during our study’s postintervention period as this period began on March 1, 2020.2Although COVID-19 may contribute to the attenuation of treatment effects postintervention, the lack of a difference between conditions postintervention is also similar to findings from past work suggesting that, after incentives to exercise are removed, treatment effects diminish substantially (Charness and Gneezy 2009,Acland and Levy 2015). Interestingly, the rate of gym visits that occurred simultaneously with a participant’s gym buddy was significantly higher for those in thestandard-rewardcondition (20.77%) compared with thetandem-rewardcondition (14.98%,χ2(1) = 6.48,p= 0.01)) in the postintervention period. Follow-up Survey We were interested not only in the impact of ourtandem-rewardcondition on exercise, but also in whether it influenced exercise for the reasons we theorized. Specifically, we sought to determine whether thetandem-rewardcondition produced greater feelings of accountability and more enjoyment of goal pursuit than thestandard-rewardcondition. To explore this, participants were offered an additional Amazon gift card for filling out a short survey following the intervention (preregistration, data, code, and stimuli available on ResearchBox). The survey assessed participants’ agreement with statements about their experience with Gym Bonus Month measured on a seven-point Likert scale (1 = very strongly disagree, 4 = neither agree nor disagree, 7 = very strongly agree). Participants responded to each of the following items (randomized): accountability (“I felt held accountable for visiting the gym during Gym Bonus Month”), enjoyment (“I enjoyed Gym Bonus Month” and “I had fun working out during Gym Bonus Month,”r= 0.75), gym buddy closeness (“I feel closer to my gym partner now than at the start of Gym Bonus Month”), payment difficulty (“I felt that getting paid during Gym Bonus Month was difficult”), and burden (“Participating in Gym Bonus Month felt like an obligation” and “Participating in Gym Bonus Month felt like a burden”). Three hundred forty-eight participants completed the survey or 44.96% of the total gym study sample (63% female,MAge= 20.77). There was a nonsignificant difference in completion rates between conditions (standard-rewardcondition=44.76%,tandem-rewardcondition = 45.15%,χ2(1) = 0.001,p= 0.971). As shown inTable 3andFigure 3, our follow-up survey provides suggestive (marginally significant) support for our proposition that participants felt more accountable for exercising and enjoyed visiting the gym more in thetandem-rewardcondition than in thestandard-rewardcondition. We also uncovered an added social benefit from thetandem-rewardapproach: participants felt significantly closer to their gym buddies at the end of the month in thetandem-rewardcondition. Finally, there was a nonsignificant difference between conditions based on the difficulty of getting paid or how burdensome it was to participate in Gym Bonus Month. That is, even though it was objectively more difficult to complete the reward requirements in thetandem-rewardcondition, participants did not report that they found this condition more burdensome. Table 3.Posttreatment Survey Responses Table 3.Posttreatment Survey Responses Note.Means and standard deviations of participants’ responses to our survey questions about their experience during Gym Bonus Month across conditions; seven indicates the strongest possible agreement with the labeled experience, and one indicates the strongest possible level of disagreement. Posttest Survey on Choice. In a preregistered posttest (https://aspredicted.org/J7N_Z1X) including undergraduate respondents from the same university whose undergraduate students populated our field study (n= 303,MAge= 20.79, 57.8% female), we tested whether individuals would prefer an incentive program with a standard or tandem reward. We stated that researchers at the university were considering running two programs in collaboration with the on-campus fitness centers for a future month. We described the two programs as follows, counterbalancing which was labeled program A versus program B (note that here, program A is ourstandard-rewardcondition and program B is ourtandem-rewardcondition): Program A: You will receive $1 every time you go to the gym. Your friend will also receive $1 every time they go to the gym. (You and your friend do not need to go to the gym at the same time in this program). Program B: You and your friend will each receive $1 every time you go to the gym, but only if you go together. That is, you both will receive $1 on days when you arrive at the gym within 30 minutes of each other (but neither of you will receive money on days when you go to the gym at different times or on days when one or both of you does not go to the gym at all). Participants had to correctly answer a comprehension check question about the programs to ensure that they understood that, in thetandem-rewardprogram, they would only be paid if they visited the gym within 30 minutes of their friend, whereas in the standard-reward program, they would be paid whether or not their friend went to the gym (and that they could still go to the gym with their friend as desired). Participants then selected which program they would prefer. The majority of participants (81.85%,z= 11.09,p< 0.001) indicated that they preferred the standard-reward scheme over thetandem-rewardscheme.3Whereas it is unsurprising that most individuals prefer the program that does not include a logistical hurdle, it suggests that students (1) may not fully anticipate the benefits of tandem rewards or (2) may appreciate them but value their benefits less than the logistical costs tandem rewards impose. Notably, we do observe that some students (roughly 18% of respondents) do potentially recognize and even prefer the benefits of tandem rewards. This preference relates to a core finding of the literature on commitment devices, which shows that some people will opt into self-imposed constraints or penalties to achieve personal goals (Ashraf et al. 2006,Rogers et al. 2014). Although our primary field experiment suggests that opting into tandem rewards over standard rewards would be an effective commitment device, most individuals either underestimate its impact or have other values that outweigh the convenience of less effective strategies. Moreover, tandem rewards might highlight a promising avenue for future commitment devices, particularly in social or collaborative contexts and when their benefits can be explained.
article.Additional Data: Pilot Data Further suggestive evidence of the benefits of conditional, tandem rewards, comes from a small, preregistered pilot study of our gym experiment in 2018 at a different university, which yielded a similar pattern of findings (see ResearchBox for full results). This pilot study employed the same experimental design as the current gym study with a smaller participant pool and also randomized the size of the incentive offered to participants per gym visit (participants received either $1 or $3 per workout, depending on condition). Although our pilot results did not reach statistical significance (n= 265 dyads;b= 0.26,SE= 0.16,p= 0.108), they are directionally consistent with the findings from the preregistered, well-powered field experiment presented here, showing a roughly 15% directional increase in gym visits for participants in thetandem-rewardcondition. The results do not differ significantly based on gender using a three-way interaction between the treatment, intervention period, and a female indicator:b= −0.223,SE= 0.237,p= 0.348. We also found that during Gym Bonus Month in this pilot study, a significantly higher rate of gym visits occurred simultaneously in thetandem-rewardcondition (56.14% of gym visits) than in thestandard-rewardcondition (28.49%;χ2(1) = 447.51,p< 0.01). These patterns add to our confidence in the robustness of our main findings. Hypothetical Scenario Study: Language Learning In an additional preregistered scenario experiment (n= 2,748;MAge= 35.26, 64.36% female; see ResearchBox for full details and results), we find further evidence (in a hypothetical setting) for the potency of a tandem reward approach. This study aimed to replicate our findings in a new context: motivating people to learn a new language. Participants were told to imagine they and a friend had both enrolled in a language class together. Those randomly assigned to imagine they had been offered tandem rewards through this program (for attending class together) reported that they would be more likely to attend (M= 4.54,SD= 1.78 on a seven-point scale from 1 = very unlikely to 7 = very likely) than those randomly assigned to imagine they had been offered standard rewards of the same magnitude by their employer for attending class regardless of their friend’s attendance (M= 4.10,SD= 1.77,t(940) = 3.76,p< 0.001,d= 0.25). As evidence for the power of accountability, participants offered tandem rewards also expected to feel significantly greater accountability (p< 0.001,d= 1.65) and concerns about social signaling (p< 0.001,d= 0.35) than those offered a standard reward and further believed the class would be more enjoyable (p< 0.001,d= 0.22).
article.Discussion: In a large, preregistered field experiment, we find that rewarding tandem goal pursuit increases goal follow-through more than does rewarding solitary goal pursuit even when tandem goal pursuit is more difficult. Participants in our field experiment went to the gym about 35% more frequently when earning a $1 reward for exercise was conditional on exercising with a friend than when they could earn the same $1 reward for exercising either on their own or with a friend. This pattern arose despite the fact that (1) it was objectively more difficult for participants to earn the same reward when they had to coordinate with a friend and (2) participants in thestandard-rewardcondition could have exercised alongside their gym buddy had they wished to do so. Tying a reward to social engagement nearly doubled the number of gym visits participants made in tandem with their gym buddies and increased self-reported feelings of both accountability and enjoyment at a marginally significant level. As an added benefit, participants reported feeling significantly closer to their gym buddy when rewards were conditional on exercising together. Our study used a convenience sample of undergraduates, which puts constraints on the generalizability of our findings. Furthermore, because of the design of the experiment, participants were asked to sign up as a pair (giving both their contact information and the contact information for a friend). This setup may have discouraged participation from individuals who either did not have a friend with whom to participate or who preferred not to participate in social activities. Indeed, recent work finds that group-based incentives may be more effective for certain subpopulations; in the context of blood donations, researchers have found that providing group rewards leads to more donations, and the donors who respond to these rewards are more likely to be older, be in a relationship, and have stronger social ties in their local area (Sun et al. 2019). Whereas we replicated our key finding in a hypothetical study that did not recruit participants in pairs, future studies exploring whether tandem rewards are effective in a broader set of study participants would be valuable. Although participants in our study were not explicitly told about other study conditions, it is possible that there was spillover or demand effects in our study whereby some participants (who did know their behavior was being studied thanks to our informed consent procedure) modified their behavior because of awareness of the alternative condition. How such an effect could bias our results is unclear, however, because it is not evident whether knowledge of the other condition would lead to an increase or decrease in the effect size. Interestingly, our follow-up choice survey reveals that individuals would have elected to be in our standard reward condition if they had been offered a choice, which suggests that, if there was any bias introduced through participants somehow becoming aware of the other study condition, it might bias against participants exerting greater effort in thetandem-rewardcondition. Moreover, further support for the efficacy of tandem rewards is found in our hypothetical study, which was designed in such a way that spillover concerns should not exist. This hypothetical study still demonstrates the superiority of tandem rewards over standard ones. We acknowledge, however, that this evidence may not completely rule out the interplay between conditions in our field study, which are unfortunately standard in field experiments with informed consent procedures (e.g.,Charness and Gneezy 2009,Royer et al. 2015,Carrera et al. 2018). Additional limitations include that, whereas we observed how often participants went to the gym, we do not know how long they spent at the gym or what kind of exercise they engaged in when they were there.4We also only tested the effect of tandem rewards on one real behavior: exercise. Our hypothetical scenario study using participants recruited from an online crowdsourcing platform and a language learning context suggests that this effect may generalize to other contexts and populations as well. However, future work testing whether tandem rewards also boost performance over standard rewards when targeting other desirable behaviors (such as employee engagement in skills trainings or other wellness programs) would be valuable. Creating an effective incentive strategy could afford employers considerable gains in fostering employee participation in such programs, simultaneously fortifying interpersonal bonds in their workforce. Our theory would also predict that the benefits of social engagement, including enjoyment and accountability, would be present for social groups larger than the dyads in our experiment. However, there are likely limits to the benefits of simultaneous goal pursuit as group size increases as the difficulty of earning rewards would also increase as the group gets larger. Future work might determine the ideal group size for simultaneous goal pursuit, taking into consideration the difficulty of a given task and whether both social accountability and enjoyment increase or decrease as the group size grows. Incentive magnitude may also affect the benefit of tandem rewards. Our primary study used small incentives though recent work corroborates that even minimal incentives can meaningfully influence behavior change (Milkman et al. 2021). The interplay between incentive magnitude and treatment condition warrants exploration; larger incentives may bolster behavioral persistence irrespective of whether they are offered for tandem or independent goal pursuit. The current work highlights an approach to boosting the impact of incentives that is financially costless to firms, also advancing our understanding of the determinants of behavior change. The findings underscore the importance of incorporating social motives into economic models of behavior (Dawes and Thaler 1988,Charness and Rabin 2002). Our findings also add to a substantial literature on the power of social influence (Cialdini and Goldstein 2004,Cialdini et al. 2006,Efferson et al. 2020) and contribute to accumulating evidence that making goal pursuit more enjoyable can add value (Milkman et al. 2014,Woolley and Fishbach 2016,Kirgios et al. 2020) as can increasing individuals’ accountability to one another (McCaul et al. 1987). Employers as well as individuals can leverage these insights by integrating social engagement into their incentive structures to foster a more engaged and productive workforce. Implications for Policy and Practice.Pursuing goals with others versus independently adds costs, such as coordination requirements and the need to compromise on individual preferences. Yet people often choose to pursue activities together. In a large field experiment, we compare the effect of offering conditional social incentives (specifically, rewards contingent on completing a task alongside a friend) to simple, standard incentives (i.e., rewards for completing the same task without a social requirement). We demonstrate that, even when it is objectively easier to complete a task on one’s own, social rewards increase goal-related behaviors in part because of nonmonetary motivators of accountability, social signaling, and enjoyment. The social reward approach not only offers a cost-effective method for enhancing goal pursuit, but also advances our understanding of social motivation. The authors thank Y. Park, T. Lee, J. Hutchings, and the Behavior Change for Good Initiative at the University of Pennsylvania for research assistance. The authors also thank U. Gneezy, G. Loewenstein, J. Kessler, and the Washington University Journal Club for feedback on the manuscript. The authors thank the UC San Diego fitness centers for partnering with them for the main study and the Washington University fitness centers for partnering with them for the pilot study. The authors declare no competing interests. Anonymized data, code, and study materials are available on ResearchBox:https://researchbox.org/2266&PEER_REVIEW_passcode=ONGMFV. 1We planned to analyze two postintervention periods: two weeks and eight weeks after our intervention. However, because of COVID-19, the on-campus gyms closed three days after our two-week postintervention period ended. 2On February 28, 2020 (the last day of Gym Bonus Month), the university’s chancellor sent the first email regarding COVID-19’s potential impact on campus gatherings and classes. On March 9, 2020, a similar email announced that the following quarter would be fully online. 3In a final attention check, participants were asked whether they would need to go with their friend to receive their reward in their chosen program, and 12 participants answered this question incorrectly. If we exclude those participants, the proportion choosing the standard reward (versus the tandem reward) does not substantially change (83.51%,z= 11.43,p< 0.001). 4Previous studies of incentivized gym behavior report that incentivizing exercise can lead to reduced pulse rate, weight, and other potential improvements on health indicators, suggesting that the incentives led to a net increase in physical activity rather than a substitution away from unincentivized activity (Charness and Gneezy 2009).
